content.text: A postmortem document that sits unread in a wiki is a finished artifact, not a learning process. The learning value of an incident comes from sharing it: surfacing the finding to people who were not in the room, embedding the insight into runbooks and training material, and measuring whether the system actually improves over time. This topic covers how to build a postmortem sharing programme that turns individual incidents into organisational knowledge.

attrs.alt: Postmortem learning flywheel diagram showing six steps: incident occurs, postmortem written, meeting and actions, learning shared, systems improve, fewer incidents — feeding back into reduced incident frequency

content.text: Why Sharing Fails (And Why It Matters)
In most engineering organisations, postmortems are written and then forgotten. Three structural failures cause this:
The document is published to a wiki that no one actively reads. Engineers only encounter it when searching for something else.
A Slack message with a link gets ignored after 20 minutes. The learning requires active engagement — reading, discussion, application.
The insights from a postmortem are not linked to runbooks, alert definitions, or onboarding material. The learning exists in a document, not in the systems that would benefit from it.

The cost of this failure is concrete: the same incident pattern recurs, the same root cause is re-discovered, and the same action items are written in a new postmortem months later. A functioning learning culture prevents this.
5-10 min slot: IC presents the most impactful SEV1
Medium — timeline + root cause + one learning point
60-90 min meeting with full document walkthrough
Monthly 30-min talk by IC or facilitator
Deep dive on one incident type or pattern
Curated set of 5-10 postmortems per team
Foundation — how this system fails and why

A monthly digest aggregates all SEV1, SEV2, and significant SEV3 incidents from the previous month into a single readable document. It is the most scalable sharing mechanism because it requires no meeting attendance and can be read asynchronously.

# Engineering Incident Digest — March 2024

This month: 2 SEV1s, 5 SEV2s, 1 near-miss. Key themes: configuration drift, provider dependency.

---

## SEV1: Inference API Latency (INC-2024-03-15-042)
**Duration:** 39 min | **Impact:** 40% of inference requests in us-east-1a

**What happened:** Memory limit configuration drift caused inference pods to OOMKill
under production load. Traffic was rerouted while the config was corrected.

**Root cause:** Helm memory limit validation checks syntax but not runtime profiles.

**Key learning:** Configuration changes need semantic validation against production
memory profiles, not just syntax checking. Staging at 20% load does not catch
memory pressure that emerges at production scale.

**Actions:** CI memory profile validation (PLAT-1102), staging load injection (PLAT-1103).

**Full postmortem:** [link]

---

## SEV2: LLM Provider Rate Limiting (INC-2024-03-22-018)
**Duration:** 22 min | **Impact:** ~15% of requests returned 429s

**What happened:** A batch processing job consumed the majority of the hourly token
quota, starving real-time inference requests.

**Root cause:** No quota isolation between batch and real-time traffic paths.

**Key learning:** Token quotas must be segmented by traffic class. Batch workloads
should run with hard caps that leave real-time headroom.

**Actions:** Per-traffic-class quota enforcement (PLAT-1108).

**Full postmortem:** [link]

---

## This month's theme: Configuration and quota management
Both SEV1s this month involved configuration that drifted from intended state and
was not caught before reaching production. This is a systemic gap in our
infrastructure change validation process. PLAT team is addressing in Q2 roadmap.

from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import NamedTuple

class DigestEntry(NamedTuple):
    incident_id: str
    severity: str
    title: str
    duration_minutes: int
    impact_summary: str
    root_cause_summary: str
    key_learning: str
    action_jira_ids: list[str]
    postmortem_url: str

def generate_monthly_digest(
    month_start: date,
    postmortem_db,
    theme_analyst,
) -> str:
    month_end = (month_start.replace(day=1) + timedelta(days=32)).replace(day=1)
    incidents = postmortem_db.query(
        start=month_start,
        end=month_end,
        severity_in=["SEV1", "SEV2"],
        status="published",
    )
    # Sort SEV1 first, then by duration descending
    incidents.sort(key=lambda i: (i.severity != "SEV1", -i.duration_minutes))

    lines = [
        f"# Engineering Incident Digest — {month_start.strftime('%B %Y')}\n",
        f"This month: {sum(1 for i in incidents if i.severity == 'SEV1')} SEV1s, "
        f"{sum(1 for i in incidents if i.severity == 'SEV2')} SEV2s.\n",
    ]
    theme = theme_analyst.identify_theme(incidents)  # LLM or manual
    if theme:
        lines.append(f"**Theme this month:** {theme}\n\n---\n")

    for inc in incidents:
        lines.extend([
            f"## {inc.severity}: {inc.title} ({inc.incident_id})\n",
            f"**Duration:** {inc.duration_minutes} min\n",
            f"**Impact:** {inc.impact_summary}\n\n",
            f"**Root cause:** {inc.root_cause_summary}\n\n",
            f"**Key learning:** {inc.key_learning}\n\n",
            f"**Full postmortem:** {inc.postmortem_url}\n\n---\n",
        ])
    return "\n".join(lines)

For significant incidents (SEV1 with wide customer impact, novel root cause, or cross-team learning), a 5-10 minute slot in the monthly engineering all-hands reaches the widest audience with the highest engagement. Structure it to maximise learning in minimum time:
Timeline overview — 3-4 key events, no jargon
Top 2-3 action items with owners and status
One thing every team can check or do differently
Open questions — keep factual, not speculative
The all-hands format works best for incidents with a

— a pattern that could affect other teams. Availability incidents caused by configuration drift, for example, are relevant to any team that manages infrastructure. A highly specific ML model behaviour incident may be better suited to a team postmortem review instead.
The highest-value knowledge transfer from a postmortem is when it updates a
runbook that responders will read under pressure in a future incident
. This makes the learning operational, not archival.
The incident revealed a diagnosis step that was missing from the runbook
The mitigation required a command or procedure that was not documented
A false lead wasted 10+ minutes — document it as 'check X only if Y is also true'
The root cause was a known failure mode that should now be in the triage checklist
A new alert was added as an action item — the runbook should link to it
Runbook PR Template for Postmortem Learning

## Runbook Update: Inference API Latency

**Postmortem:** INC-2024-03-15-042
**Learning incorporated:** Memory limit configuration drift as OOM cause

### Changes

Added to **Triage** section:
```
3b. Check for memory limit drift (new — from INC-2024-03-15-042):
    kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n inference | grep -A3 'Limits:'
    Compare to actual memory use:
    kubectl top pod -n inference
    If configured limit < 80% of actual usage → OOM risk. Cordon and redeploy.
```

Added to **Known false leads** section:
```
- High network latency on provider dashboard does NOT mean OOM is not the cause.
  Check pod memory before investigating provider (INC-2024-03-15-042).
```
A learning culture is not self-sustaining — it requires measurement to detect when it is degrading. Track these metrics quarterly:
Actions closed by due date / total
Incidents with same root cause as prior incident within 90 days / total
Unique opens / engineers on distribution list
Days from incident to runbook update
> 30 days — learning not embedded in ops
Flat or increasing despite active postmortems

from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def compute_learning_culture_metrics(
    postmortem_db,
    incident_db,
    period_days: int = 90,
) -> dict:
    end = datetime.utcnow()
    start = end - timedelta(days=period_days)
    incidents = incident_db.query(start=start, end=end, severity_in=["SEV1", "SEV2"])
    postmortems = postmortem_db.query(start=start, end=end)

    # Publication rate
    on_time = sum(
        1 for pm in postmortems
        if (pm.published_at - pm.incident_resolved_at).days <= 5
    )
    publication_rate = on_time / len(postmortems) if postmortems else 0

    # Action item completion rate
    actions = [a for pm in postmortems for a in pm.action_items]
    on_time_actions = sum(
        1 for a in actions
        if a.closed_at and (a.closed_at - a.created_at).days <= 30
    )
    action_completion_rate = on_time_actions / len(actions) if actions else 0

    # Repeat incident rate
    root_causes = [pm.root_cause_tags for pm in postmortems]
    seen = set()
    repeats = 0
    for tags in root_causes:
        for tag in tags:
            if tag in seen:
                repeats += 1
                break
            seen.add(tag)
    repeat_rate = repeats / len(postmortems) if postmortems else 0

    # MTTR trend
    mttr_values = [
        (inc.resolved_at - inc.declared_at).seconds / 60
        for inc in incidents
    ]
    median_mttr = sorted(mttr_values)[len(mttr_values) // 2] if mttr_values else 0

    return {
        "period_days": period_days,
        "total_incidents": len(incidents),
        "postmortem_publication_rate": round(publication_rate, 3),
        "action_completion_rate_30d": round(action_completion_rate, 3),
        "repeat_incident_rate": round(repeat_rate, 3),
        "median_mttr_minutes": round(median_mttr, 1),
    }
Metrics track the culture, but leadership behaviour
it. Engineers watch what managers do during and after incidents more carefully than they watch formal policies. These leadership signals matter most:
Manager attends postmortem meeting without speaking
Post-mortems are important enough for my time
Manager skips all postmortems — signals they don't matter
Leadership praises fast detection and honest timelines
Manager asks 'how did this happen?' with a tone that implies blame
IC who ran a bad incident is asked to present at all-hands
Incidents are learning opportunities, not marks against you
Action items appear on team roadmap alongside features
Action items in a separate 'tech debt' list that never gets done
Leadership cites past postmortems in design reviews
The organisation remembers and uses its learning
Postmortems cited only to blame — 'this happened before'
At scale, the most valuable use of the postmortem archive is
: when a new incident starts, can a responder quickly find all prior incidents with the same root cause pattern? This requires tagging postmortems consistently.

# Postmortem tagging schema
postmortem:
  id: INC-2024-03-15-042
  severity: SEV1
  duration_minutes: 39

  # Incident classification tags (use standard taxonomy)
  type: availability
  component: inference-api
  region: us-east-1a

  # Root cause tags (controlled vocabulary)
  root_cause_tags:
    - configuration-drift
    - memory-limit
    - ci-validation-gap

  # Detection / response tags
  detection_method: automated-alert
  mttd_minutes: 2
  mttr_minutes: 39

  # Action item tags
  action_types: [prevent, detect]
  teams_owning_actions: [ml-platform, observability]

A search like 'root_cause_tags:configuration-drift component:inference-api' returns all prior incidents with the same pattern, giving a new IC immediate context and a pre-built set of diagnostic steps from the prior runbook updates.
Beautiful documents written, never read, action items never tracked
No organisational learning; repeat incidents every quarter
Shared only with the team that caused the incident
Adjacent teams repeat the same mistakes independently
Document says 'process failure' but the narrative is clearly about one person
Psychological safety erodes; people stop being honest in postmortems
Post-mortems produce 10+ actions, none have owners, none are tracked
Engineers stop taking actions seriously; culture degrades
All postmortem energy goes to faster detection; resilience and prevention ignored
Faster alerting on recurring incidents — MTTR unchanged
Each team has its own postmortem wiki and format
Patterns that span teams are never identified; no org-level learning
A postmortem published to a wiki but not actively shared produces no organisational learning
Use multiple channels scaled to audience: monthly digest for breadth, all-hands for significant SEV1s, team reviews for depth
The highest-value sharing mechanism is updating runbooks — it makes learning operational, not archival
Track learning culture health with: publication rate, action completion rate, repeat incident rate, digest read rate, MTTR trend
Leadership signals matter more than formal policies: attending postmortems, praising honest timelines, putting actions on the roadmap
Tag postmortems with a controlled vocabulary to enable search — a new IC should be able to find all prior incidents with the same root cause in under 30 seconds
Avoid the six anti-patterns: postmortem theatre, private postmortems, blame laundering, action item graveyard, MTTD-only focus, no cross-team visibility
